1: theme The psalm opens with a vow of unbroken blessing: I will bless the LORD at all times; his praise shall continually be in my mouth. The perfect worshiper who embodies this posture is Christ himself, the Son who eternally blesses the Father. The redeemed share this continuous praise because they are in him (Hebrews 13:15 — the sacrifice of praise through Christ).
2: theme My soul makes its boast in the LORD; let the humble hear and be glad. Paul's theology of boasting grounds in this tradition: Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord (1 Corinthians 1:31; 2 Corinthians 10:17, quoting Jeremiah 9:24). Christ is the one in whom all legitimate boasting is grounded — he is the LORD in whom the soul rightly glories.
3: theme Oh, magnify the LORD with me, and let us exalt his name together. The communal invitation to praise is realized in the gathering of the church around the risen Christ. He is the center of the assembly (Matthew 18:20), and the congregation's corporate voice magnifies the name he has made known (John 17:26).
4: shadow I sought the LORD and he answered me and delivered me from all my fears. In Gethsemane Christ sought the Father with loud cries and tears; Hebrews 5:7 records that he was heard because of his reverence. The deliverance came not by removing the cup but through resurrection — the ultimate answer to the righteous sufferer's seeking.
5: shadow Those who look to him are radiant, and their faces shall never be ashamed. The Transfiguration discloses what looking to Christ produces: his face shone like the sun (Matthew 17:2). Paul extends this: beholding the glory of the Lord, believers are transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another (2 Corinthians 3:18).
6: shadow This poor man cried, and the LORD heard him and saved him out of all his troubles. The poor man who cries is the archetype Christ inhabits: though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor (2 Corinthians 8:9). He is the afflicted righteous one whose cry the Father hears — and the resurrection is God's saving answer.
7: shadow The angel of the LORD encamps around those who fear him and delivers them. The angel of the LORD in the Hebrew Bible often represents a divine presence that cannot be distinguished from YHWH himself (Genesis 16:13; Exodus 3:2). Early Christian interpreters read these theophanies as pre-incarnate appearances of the Son — the Deliverer who encamps becomes the one who pitched his tent among us (John 1:14).
8: direct Oh, taste and see that the LORD is good! Blessed is the man who takes refuge in him! 1 Peter 2:3 applies this verse directly to Christ: if indeed you have tasted that the Lord is good — addressing newly baptized believers. Peter identifies the LORD of Psalm 34 with the Lord Jesus, making the invitation to taste an invitation to trust Christ himself.
9: theme Oh, fear the LORD, you his saints, for those who fear him have no lack! Holy reverence before God is christologically grounded: Christ himself modeled perfect fear of the LORD (Isaiah 11:2-3; Hebrews 5:7 — heard because of his reverence), and believers approach the Father with reverence and awe through him (Hebrews 12:28-29).
10: theme The young lions suffer want and hunger, but those who seek the LORD lack no good thing. The bread of life discourse answers this promise directly: I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst (John 6:35). Christ is the one in whom seekers of the LORD find every good thing.
11: shadow Come, O children, listen to me; I will teach you the fear of the LORD. The teacher-gathering-children pattern reaches its fulfillment in Christ's invitation: Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest (Matthew 11:28-30). He is the divine teacher who gathers children and forms them in the way of the LORD.
12: direct What man is there who desires life and loves many days, that he may see good? 1 Peter 3:10-12 quotes Psalms 34:12-16 as a block, applying it to Christian conduct under Christ's lordship. Peter's citation transforms the rhetorical question into instruction for the community that belongs to the Lord Jesus.
13: direct Keep your tongue from evil and your lips from speaking deceit. Part of the 1 Peter 3:10-12 citation block. Peter addresses this to believers who follow the example of Christ who committed no sin, neither was deceit found in his mouth (1 Peter 2:22, citing Isaiah 53:9) — the Servant becomes the model the redeemed imitate.
14: direct Turn away from evil and do good; seek peace and pursue it. Part of the 1 Peter 3:10-12 citation block. The peace-seeking life is grounded in the one who is our peace (Ephesians 2:14) — Christ who broke down the wall of hostility becomes the foundation for the pursuit of shalom.
15: direct The eyes of the LORD are toward the righteous and his ears toward their cry. Part of the 1 Peter 3:10-12 citation block. The righteous one to whom the LORD's eyes turn is supremely Christ himself — the perfectly righteous man whose cry in Gethsemane and on the cross was heard by the Father (Hebrews 5:7).
16: direct The face of the LORD is against those who do evil, to cut off the memory of them from the earth. Final verse of the 1 Peter 3:10-12 citation block. The judgment the wicked face is administered through Christ to whom all judgment has been given (John 5:22); those who do evil face the one whose face shines in glory (Revelation 1:16).
17: shadow When the righteous cry for help, the LORD hears and delivers them out of all their troubles. Hebrews 5:7 applies the pattern to Christ: In the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard. Christ is the righteous one who cries and is delivered — through resurrection.
18: revelation of God The LORD is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit. This nearness of God to the crushed is most fully embodied in the Incarnation: God draws near in flesh to weep at Lazarus's tomb (John 11:35), to touch lepers, to receive the broken. Immanuel — God-with-us — is the ultimate answer to the crushed spirit's need.
19: shadow Many are the afflictions of the righteous, but the LORD delivers him out of them all. Christ is the afflicted righteous one: he was despised and rejected by men, a man of sorrows and acquainted with grief (Isaiah 53:3). But the Father delivers him out of them all — the resurrection is the definitive deliverance that validates the pattern promised here.
20: direct He keeps all his bones; not one of them is broken. John 19:36 applies this verse directly to the crucifixion: These things took place that the Scripture might be fulfilled: 'Not one of his bones will be broken.' When the soldiers came to break the legs of the crucified, they found Jesus already dead — fulfilling Psalms 34:20 to the letter.
21: shadow Evil will slay the wicked, and those who hate the righteous will be condemned. The cosmic reversal at the cross and resurrection: sin is condemned in the flesh (Romans 8:3), death is swallowed up in victory (1 Corinthians 15:54-55), and those who rejected the righteous one face their own condemnation. The evil that struck Christ ultimately destroys only the wicked.
22: theme The LORD redeems the life of his servants; none of those who take refuge in him will be condemned. The atonement as redemption: In him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses (Ephesians 1:7). The promise of no condemnation is Paul's great declaration in Romans 8:1 — grounded in what Christ accomplished.
